_meta.cycle_disposition_reason: Multiple converging AMOC precursor studies, C3S El Niño development confirmation, and quantified carbon cycle feedback constitute material Earth system signal escalation.

lead_signal.headline: Gulf Stream abrupt northward displacement identified as AMOC collapse early-warning indicator in peer-reviewed study
lead_signal.body: A 2026 Communications Earth & Environment paper found that AMOC weakening gradually shifts the Gulf Stream near Cape Hatteras northward, followed by an abrupt northward displacement of approximately 219 km within two years, occurring decades before simulated AMOC collapse. Satellite altimetry data from 1993 to 2024 and subsurface temperature observations from 1965 to 2024 both confirm a statistically significant northward Gulf Stream trend at Cape Hatteras, providing observational grounding for the modelled precursor. This constitutes a new Tier 2 early-warning indicator for AMOC tipping, reinforcing the accelerating-slowdown trajectory assessment.

planetary_status_snapshot.lead_signal: AMOC carbon cycle feedback quantified at 47 to 83 ppm additional atmospheric CO2 upon collapse

tipping_point_tracker.key_indicator: Gulf Stream northward displacement at Cape Hatteras; observational constraints project approximately 50 percent AMOC weakening by 2100
tipping_point_tracker.cascade_risk: AMOC collapse would trigger Arctic cooling of approximately 7 degrees C, Antarctic warming of approximately 6 degrees C, and disrupt West African monsoon and European agriculture
tipping_point_tracker.cascade_chain: AMOC weakening shifts Gulf Stream northward, followed by abrupt 219 km displacement within two years, occurring decades before collapse; collapse releases 47 to 83 ppm additional atmospheric CO2, adding approximately 0.2 degrees C global warming; precipitation shifts disrupt Sahel, West Africa, South Asia food and water security; displacement and conflict risk escalates
tipping_point_tracker.source: Communications Earth & Environment 2026; Science Advances 2026; Carbon Brief AMOC Explainer
tipping_point_tracker.asymmetric: Multiple converging Tier 2 studies elevate AMOC tipping trajectory to dominant Earth system signal; carbon cycle feedback loop previously underexplored; Gulf Stream displacement constitutes new early-warning indicator

key_judgments.judgment: AMOC tipping trajectory has escalated to the dominant Earth system signal of the current reporting cycle, with multiple converging Tier 2 studies identifying new early-warning indicators and quantifying previously underexplored carbon cycle feedback loops
El Niño development confirmed by C3S Tier 1 data opens bleaching window for coral reefs and elevates drought precursor risk in Central America Dry Corridor, Southeast Asia, and northern South America

AMOC collapse carbon cycle feedback of 47 to 83 ppm additional atmospheric CO2 creates a positive feedback loop between tipping system and climate change planetary boundary, materially altering long-run carbon pricing and stranded asset calculations
